metadata.summary: Configure zero-downtime Kubernetes rolling updates with maxUnavailable/maxSurge tuning, minReadySeconds, rollback procedure, and pre/post-update verification.

content.title: Kubernetes Rolling Update Strategy
content.summary: Rolling update is the default and safest Kubernetes deployment strategy for most production workloads. It gradually replaces old pods with new ones, keeping the service available throughout. Zero-downtime requires maxUnavailable: 0 plus a properly configured readiness probe and minReadySeconds delay. kubectl rollout undo enables fast rollback to any revision in history.

why.p: The Recreate strategy terminates all old pods before starting new ones — acceptable only in development when running two versions simultaneously is impossible. Rolling update avoids this downtime window, but naive settings (maxUnavailable 25% with no readiness probe) still cause brief outages when pods serve traffic before they are ready. The zero-downtime formula — maxUnavailable: 0, maxSurge: 1, minReadySeconds, readiness probe — is the correct baseline for all production HTTP services.

list.item: Updating image versions, configuration, or environment variables on any Deployment.
Tuning rollout speed vs. availability for high-replica Deployments.
Debugging a stuck or failed rollout.
Executing or documenting a rollback procedure.
High-risk releases where you need percentage-based traffic control and automated metric analysis — use canary/Argo Rollouts (k8s-canary-progressive).
StatefulSets requiring controlled partition rollouts — use updateStrategy.rollingUpdate.partition.
Development environments where downtime is acceptable and Recreate is simpler.
Rolling Update: zero downtime, kubectl rollout undo for rollback, no traffic control. Low complexity. Default.
Recreate: causes downtime (all pods terminated before new ones start), simple rollback. Use only when two versions cannot run simultaneously.
Blue-Green: zero downtime, instant rollback via service selector switch, binary traffic control. Medium complexity.
Canary: zero downtime, gradual rollback, percentage-based traffic. High complexity — see k8s-canary-progressive.

section.name: zero-downtime-config
section.p: Three settings control the rolling update budget. maxUnavailable: maximum pods that can be down relative to desired replicas (0 = never reduce below desired count). maxSurge: extra pods allowed above desired replicas during the update. minReadySeconds: seconds a new pod must be Running and passing readiness before it is considered Available — catching early failures before the old pod is terminated.

rule.statement: For zero-downtime rolling updates set maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 1 (or 25% for large Deployments). Set minReadySeconds to 10-30s to catch early probe failures before old pods are removed. The readiness probe MUST validate that the new version can serve traffic.
rule.rationale: maxUnavailable: 0 guarantees full capacity throughout the update. Without minReadySeconds a crashing new pod can be marked Available before its first restart, causing a brief window where old pods are removed and the new pod has already crashed. The readiness probe is the actual gate — if it is missing or returns 200 immediately regardless of state, minReadySeconds provides no protection.

example.title: Zero-downtime rolling update
example.code: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment spec: replicas: 3 strategy: type: RollingUpdate rollingUpdate: maxUnavailable: 0 # Never reduce below desired replicas maxSurge: 1 # Add one pod at a time minReadySeconds: 10 # Wait 10s before considering pod ready
example.note: Update sequence: [v1,v1,v1] → [v1,v1,v1,v2-starting] → [v1,v1,v2] → [v1,v1,v2,v2-starting] → ... → [v2,v2,v2]. Three pods always available.

example.title: Fast rolling update for large Deployments
example.code: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment spec: replicas: 10 strategy: type: RollingUpdate rollingUpdate: maxUnavailable: 2 # Allow 2 pods down maxSurge: 3 # Allow 3 extra pods minReadySeconds: 5
example.note: Accepts brief reduction to 8/10 pods in exchange for faster rollout. Use when cluster capacity allows surge and brief under-capacity is acceptable.

example.title: Missing minReadySeconds with fast-crashing app
example.code: strategy: rollingUpdate: maxUnavailable: 1 maxSurge: 1 # No minReadySeconds — new pod marked Available immediately # If it crashes after 2s, old pod is already gone
example.note: A new pod that passes its startup probe but fails 3 seconds later will be marked Available before the crash, allowing the old pod to be terminated and creating a brief outage.

checklist.check: HPA behaviour understood (consider disabling during update if metric spikes expected).
minReadySeconds set to catch early failures.
Monitor rollout status: kubectl rollout status.
Watch pod transitions: kubectl get pods -w.
Check error logs: kubectl logs -f deployment/name.

section.name: rollback-procedure
section.p: When to rollback: error rate exceeds threshold (more than 1% increase), P99 latency exceeds threshold (more than 10% increase), crash loop detected, critical functionality broken, security vulnerability discovered.

example.title: Full rollback procedure
example.code:
# 1. Check current status kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>
# 2. View revision history kubectl rollout history deployment/<name>
# 3. Execute rollback kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>
# 4. Monitor rollback kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>
# 5. Verify pods kubectl get pods -l app=<name>
# 6. Test application curl https://app.example.com/health
